title: Confidence intervals
text: compute confidence intervals, first fit model using ameras, use confint method attach confidence intervals. Several types confidence intervals supported, supplied type argument confint, see . confint called methods containing least one RC, ERC, MCML least one FMA BMA, type vector length 2 one method RC, ERC MCML one FMA BMA.

what: Regression calibration, extended regression calibration, and Monte Carlo maximum likelihood
title: Confidence intervals
text: (extended) regression calibration Monte Carlo maximum likelihood, Wald profile likelihood intervals can obtained. parameter transformation \mathbf\theta = h(\mathbf\eta) used, type="wald.transformed" yields CI significance level \alpha h(\mathbf\eta \pm z_{1-\alpha/2} \mathbf V) z_{1-\alpha/2} 1-\alpha/2-quantile standard normal distribution \mathbf V vector standard deviations estimated using inverse Hessian matrix (returned optim), type="wald.orig" uses delta method obtain CI h(\mathbf\eta)\pm z_{1-\alpha/2} \mathbf V_* \mathbf V_* vector standard deviations estimated using J H^{-1} J^T J Jacobian transformation (obtained transform.jacobian) H Hessian. transformation used, type="wald.orig" used. third option type="proflik", uses profile likelihood compute confidence bounds. , profile log (partial) likelihood parameter \theta_p defined      PL_p (\theta_p^*) = \max_{\mathbf\theta: \theta_p = \theta_p^*} \ell (\mathbf \theta),  \ell log (partial) likelihood. Next, profile confidence intervals (\theta_p^l, \theta_p^h) obtained parameter \theta_p significance level \alpha solving -2 \{PL_p(\theta_p^*) - \ell(\hat{\mathbf{\theta}})\}=\chi^2_{1,1-\alpha} using bisection method, \hat{\mathbf{\theta}} maximum likelihood estimate. Note profile likelihoods computationally intensive obtain. reason, confint offers option determine exposure-related parameters, default setting. obtain profile likelihood intervals parameters, use parm = "". illustrate, determine three types confidence intervals regression calibration analysis using example data, using linear excess relative risk model default exponential transformation (see Parameter transformations).

methods=c("RC")) #> Fitting RC  fit.ameras.waldorig <- confint(fit.ameras, type="wald.orig") fit.ameras.waldtransformed <- confint(fit.ameras, type="wald.transformed") fit.ameras.proflik <- title: Confidence intervals
text: frequentist Bayesian model averaging methods, options percentile uses equal-tailed quantiles, hpd computes highest posterior density intervals using HPDinterval coda package, using either FMA samples Bayesian posterior samples. , use example data illustrate.

text: functionality package included function ameras. vignette demonstrates basic functionality generated output. information obtaining confidence/credible intervals, see Confidence intervals.

title: Fitting models and displaying output
text: Models specified formulas form Y~dose(dose_expression, model="ERR", deg=1, modifier=M1+M2)+X1+X2. dose_expression specifies dose realization columns parsed eval_select tidyselect package. Useful examples D1:D1000 doses sequence columns sequential names D1-D1000, all_of(dosevars) dosevars vector names dose columns. , model specifies, non-Gaussian families, whether use exponential dose-response model (model="EXP"), linear-exponential model (model="LINEXP") linear ERR model (model="ERR"). Next, deg used specify whether quadratic dose term (deg=2) (deg=1) estimated exponential linear ERR dose-response model. modifier term optional used specify binary effect modification variables. Finally, X1 X2 represent optional additional covariates, can include factor variables interactions X1*X2. Note interactions modifier term allowed, e.g. M1*M2. deg, modifier, model supplied, defaults deg=1, effect modifiers, model="ERR".

title: Fitting models and displaying output
text: included example dataset contains 3,000 individuals 10 exposure replicates V1-V10, binary covariates X1 X2 effect modifiers M1 M2, outcomes types (Y.gaussian, Y.binomial, Y.poisson, status, time, Y.multinomial, Y.clogit, setnr).

title: Fitting models and displaying output
text: Proportional hazards regression uses syntax similar survival package, models specified using formulas Surv(exit, status) Surv(entry, exit, status) left hand side. Note BMA fits piecewise constant baseline hazard h0 proportional hazards model directly supported. default, observed time interval divided 10 intervals using quantiles observed event times among cases. number intervals can specified prophaz.numints.BMA argument. BMA output contains prophaz.numints.BMA+1 cutpoints defining intervals addition h0. , first fit models including quadratic exposure term setting deg=2. BMA output now contains intervals piecewise constant baseline hazards, corresponding estimates h0: Without quadratic term (deg=1):

title: Relative risk models
text: non-Gaussian families, three relative risk models main exposure supported, usual exponential model RR_i=\exp(\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2+ \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m1}D_i + \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m2} D_i^2), linear(-quadratic) excess relative risk (ERR) model RR_i= 1+\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2 + \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta_{m1}}D_i + \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m2}D_i^2, linear-exponential model  RR_i= 1+(\beta_1 + \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m1}) D_i \exp\{(\beta_2+  \mathbf{M}_i^T \mathbf{\beta}_{m2})D_i\}.  vignette illustrates fitting three models using regression calibration logistic regression, syntax applies settings.

title: Parameter transformations
text: included function transform1 applies exponential transformation f(\theta_i)=\exp(\theta_i)+LB_i one multiple components parameter vector \mathbf \theta, LB_i lower limits can different component. particular, vector indices parameters transformed vector corresponding lower bounds LB can supplied arguments index.t lowlimit, respectively, resulting transformed parameters f(\theta_i)=\exp(\theta_i)+\text{LB}_i. particular, transform1 transform1.jacobian defined follows:
code: transform1 <- function(params, index.t=1:length(params), lowlimit=rep(0,length(index.t)),                         boundcheck=FALSE, boundtol=1e-3, ...){      if(length(index.t)!=length(lowlimit))      stop("Length mismatch between index.t and lowlimit")   if(any(!(index.t %in% 1:length(params))))      stop("Incorrect indices for transformation specified")   params[index.t] <- exp(params[index.t]) + lowlimit   if(boundcheck){     if(any(params[index.t]-lowlimit < boundtol))        warning(paste0("WARNING: one or multiple parameter estimates within ", boundtol, " of          lower bounds. Try different bounds or starting values."))   }   return(params) }  transform1.jacobian <- function(params, index.t=1:length(params), ...){    if(any(!(index.t %in% 1:length(params))))      stop("Incorrect indices for transformation specified")   grad <- rep(1, length(params))   grad[index.t] <- exp(params[index.t])   if(length(params)>1){     return(diag(grad))   } else{     return(matrix(grad))   } }

title: Parameter transformations
text: wish supply transformation, helpful start definition transform1. also important keep following mind: minimum, function take arguments params ..., params full parameter vector. Parameters specific order (see ), case doubt always possible run ameras default settings verify correct order result. Extra arguments can used supplied calling ameras Optional: boundcheck argument function, logical. transforming parameters optimization, transformation called boundcheck=TRUE. boundcheck argument function, ignored. Also define Jacobian transformation, keeping mind point 1 . See definition transform1 example apply transformation specific parameters, use extra arguments. example, suppose instead exponential transformation transform1, parameters \beta_1 \beta_2 wish use sigmoid transformation f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow (,b) given f_{a_i,b_i}(\theta_i)= a_i + (b_i-a_i) \frac{1}{1+\exp(-\theta_i)}. , using transform1 starting point, can define transformation follows (note since \mathbf{} \mathbf{b} act bounds, use updated bound check): Next, noting \mathrm{d}f_{a_i,b_i}/d\theta_i = (b_i-a_i)\exp(-\theta_i)/\{1+\exp(-\theta_i) \}^2, can define Jacobian follows, using transform1.jacobian starting point: Now let us try transformation example data using regression calibration linear-quadratic ERR model. Note parameters returned transformation, difference using transform1 transform.sigmoid. First, fit model using sigmoid transformation: Next default settings, using transform1: expected, difference results.
code: transform.sigmoid <- function(params, index.t=1:length(params), a=rep(0,length(index.t)),                                b=rep(1,length(index.t)), boundcheck=FALSE, boundtol=1e-3, ...){      if(length(index.t)!=length(a) | length(index.t) != length(b))      stop("Length mismatch between index.t, a, and b")   if(any(!(index.t %in% 1:length(params))))      stop("Incorrect indices for transformation specified")      params[index.t] <- a + (b-a) * 1/(1+exp(-1*params[index.t]))   if(boundcheck){     if(any( (params[index.t]-a < boundtol) | (b-params[index.t] < boundtol)))        warning(paste0("WARNING: one or multiple parameter estimates within ", boundtol,        " of bounds. Try different bounds or starting values."))   }   return(params) } transform.sigmoid.jacobian <- function(params, index.t=1:length(params),                                         a=rep(0,length(index.t)), b=rep(1,length(index.t)), ...){    if(length(index.t)!=length(a) | length(index.t) != length(b))      stop("Length mismatch between index.t, a, and b")   if(any(!(index.t %in% 1:length(params))))      stop("Incorrect indices for transformation specified")   grad <- rep(1, length(params))   grad[index.t] <- (b-a)*exp(-1*params[index.t])/(1+exp(-1*params[index.t]))^2   if(length(params)>1){     return(diag(grad))   } else{     return(matrix(grad))   } } data(data, package="ameras") fit.ameras.sigmoid <- ameras(Y.binomial~dose(V1:V10, deg=2, model="ERR")+X1+X2, data=data,                              family="binomial", methods="RC", transform=transform.sigmoid,                              transform.jacobian=transform.sigmoid.jacobian, index.t=4:5) #> Fitting RC summary(fit.ameras.sigmoid) #> Call: #> ameras(formula = Y.binomial ~ dose(V1:V10, deg = 2, model = "ERR") +  #>     X1 + X2, data = data, family = "binomial", methods = "RC",  #>     transform = transform.sigmoid, transform.jacobian = transform.sigmoid.jacobian,  #>     index.t = 4:5) #>  #> Total run time: 0.6 seconds #>  #> Runtime in seconds by method: #>  #>  Method Runtime #>      RC     0.6 #>  #> Summary of coefficients by method: #>  #>  Method         Term Estimate      SE #>      RC  (Intercept) -0.87358 0.09758 #>      RC           X1  0.44587 0.07672 #>      RC           X2 -0.33552 0.09610 #>      RC         dose  0.04875 0.21281 #>      RC dose_squared  0.28764 0.08100 #>  #> Note: confidence intervals not yet computed. Use confint() to add them. fit.ameras.transform1 <- ameras(Y.binomial~dose(V1:V10, deg=2, model="ERR")+X1+X2, data=data,                              family="binomial", methods="RC") #> Fitting RC summary(fit.ameras.transform1) #> Call: #> ameras(formula = Y.binomial ~ dose(V1:V10, deg = 2, model = "ERR") +  #>     X1 + X2, data = data, family = "binomial", methods = "RC") #>  #> Total run time: 0.3 seconds #>  #> Runtime in seconds by method: #>  #>  Method Runtime #>      RC     0.3 #>  #> Summary of coefficients by method: #>  #>  Method         Term Estimate      SE #>      RC  (Intercept) -0.87359 0.09759 #>      RC           X1  0.44587 0.07672 #>      RC           X2 -0.33552 0.09610 #>      RC         dose  0.04878 0.21283 #>      RC dose_squared  0.28763 0.08100 #>  #> Note: confidence intervals not yet computed. title: Analyze Multiple Exposure Realizations in Association Studies
text: goal ameras provide user-friendly interface analyze association studies multiple replicates noisy exposure using variety methods. ameras supports continuous, count, binary, multinomial, right-censored time--event outcomes. binary outcomes, nested case-control design also accommodated. Besides common exponential relative risk model RR=\exp(\beta D) exposure-outcome association noisy exposure D, linear excess relative risk RR=1+\beta D linear-exponential excess relative risk models RR=1+\beta_1 D \exp(\beta_2 D) can used.

what: Analyze multiple exposure realizations in association studies — ameras-package
title: Analyze multiple exposure realizations in association studies — ameras-package
text: Analyze association studies multiple realizations noisy uncertain exposure. can obtained e.g. two-dimensional Monte Carlo dosimetry system (Simon et al 2015 <doi:10.1667/RR13729.1>) characterize exposure uncertainty.     Methods include regression calibration (Carroll et al. 2006 doi:10.1201/9781420010138 ), extended     regression calibration (Little et al. 2023 doi:10.1038/s41598-023-42283-y ), Monte Carlo maximum     likelihood (Stayner et al. 2007 doi:10.1667/RR0677.1 ), frequentist model averaging (Kwon et al. 2023 doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0290498 ),     Bayesian model averaging (Kwon et al. 2016 doi:10.1002/sim.6635 ). Supported model families     Gaussian, binomial, multinomial, Poisson, proportional hazards, conditional logistic.

title: Analyze multiple exposure realizations in association studies — ameras-package

title: Analyze multiple exposure realizations — ameras
text: Fit regression models accounting exposure uncertainty using multiple Monte Carlo exposure realizations. Six outcome model families supported. first Gaussian family continuous outcomes, $$Y_i \sim N(\mu_i, \sigma^2),$$ \(\mu_i = \alpha_0 + \bm X_i^T \bm \alpha +\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2 + \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m1}D_i + \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m2}D_i^2\). \(\bm X_i\) covariates, \(D_i\) exposure measurement error, \(\bm M_i\) binary effect modifiers. quadratic exposure terms effect modification optional. non-Gaussian families, three relative risk models main exposure supported, usual exponential \(RR_i=\exp(\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2+ \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m1}D_i + \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m2} D_i^2)\) linear excess relative risk (ERR) model \(RR_i= 1+\beta_1 D_i+\beta_2 D_i^2 + \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m1}D_i + \bm M_i^T \bm \beta_{m2}D_i^2\), quadratic effect modification terms optional. Finally, linear-exponential relative risk model \(RR_i= 1+(\beta_1 + \bm{M}_i^T \bm{\beta}_{m1}) D_i \exp\{(\beta_2+  \bm{M}_i^T \bm{\beta}_{m2})D_i\}\) supported. second supported family logistic regression binary outcomes, probabilities $$p_i/(1-p_i)=RR_i\exp(\alpha_0+\bm X_i^T \bm \alpha).$$ Third Poisson regression counts, $$Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\mu_i),$$ \(\mu_i=RR_i \exp(\alpha_0 +\bm X_i^T \bm  \alpha)\times \text{offset}_i\) optional offset. Fourth proportional hazards regression time--event data, hazard function $$h(t) = h_0(t)RR_i\exp(\bm X_i^T \bm \alpha),$$ \(h_0\) baseline hazard. Fifth multinomial logistic regression categorical outcome \(Z>2\) outcome categories, last category referent category (.e., \(\alpha_{0,Z}=\bm \alpha_{Z}=\beta_{1,Z}=\beta_{2,Z}=\bm \beta_{m1,Z} = \bm \beta_{m2,Z}=0\)): $$P(Y_i=z)=RR_i\exp(\alpha_{0,z}+\bm X_i^T \bm \alpha_{z})/\{1+\sum_{s=1}^{Z-1} RR_i\exp(\alpha_{0,s}+\bm X_i^T \bm \alpha_{s})\}$$ Sixth conditional logistic regression matched case control data, $$P\left(Y_i = 1, Y_k = 0 \forall k \neq \bigg| \sum_{\\mathcal{R}} Y_i = 1\right) = RR_i\exp(\bm X_i^T \bm \alpha)/\{\sum_{k \\mathcal{R}}RR_k\exp(\bm X_k^T \bm \alpha)\},$$ \(\mathcal{R}\) matched set corresponding individual \(\). Methods include regression calibration (Carroll et al. 2006 doi:10.1201/9781420010138 ), extended regression calibration (Little et al. 2023 doi:10.1038/s41598-023-42283-y ), Monte Carlo maximum likelihood (Stayner et al. 2007 doi:10.1667/RR0677.1 ), frequentist model averaging (Kwon et al. 2023 doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0290498 ), Bayesian model averaging (Kwon et al. 2016 doi:10.1002/sim.6635 ).

RC, ERC, MCML  following additional output included: vcov covariance matrix full parameter vector. optim list object results returned optim. Components par (raw parameters applying transformation applicable), hessian (Hessian matrix par), convergence (convergence code 0 indicating convergence 1 indicating maximal number iterations reached), counts (number likelihood function evaluations used optimization). loglik log-likelihood value optimum. RC ERC, output additionally contains: ERC logical, whether output ERC (ERC=TRUE) RC (ERC=FALSE). BMA  output additionally contains: samples MCMC posterior samples, obtained nimble. list object nchains.BMA components, named matrix samples one chain rows, columns corresponding model parameters. Rhat data frame two columns, Rhat n.eff. first  column contains Gelman-Rubin statistics \(\hat R \geq 1\) can used assess convergence MCMC chains. value 1 indicates good convergence values \(>1.05\) indicate poor convergence. effective sample size n.eff measure many independent samples auto-correlated MCMC samples correspond . low effective sample size indicates high correlations /poor mixing. included.replicates indices replicate exposures included obtain results. prophaz.timepoints family="prophaz", time points defining intervals estimated baseline hazards constant; prophaz.numints.BMA + 1 time points covering interval (min(entry), max(exit)), based quantiles among observed event times. Finally, FMA output additionally contains: samples samples generated normal distributions associated dose replicate. included.samples total number samples included. included.replicates indices replicate exposures included obtain results. Fits without valid variance estimate (.e., non-invertible Hessian inverse positive definite) reach maximal number iterations without convergence filtered used obtain results. class amerasfit supports methods print, coef, confint, summary, traceplot.

CI.upper upper confidence bound, confidence intervals           computed via confint. pval.lower p-value associated lower bound profile likelihood           CI, assess validity obtained bound. included           profile likelihood confidence intervals computed via           confint. pval.upper p-value associated upper bound profile likelihood           CI, assess validity obtained bound. included           profile likelihood confidence intervals computed via           confint. Rhat Gelman-Rubin convergence diagnostic, included           BMA results present. Values 1.05 indicate potential convergence problems. n.eff effective sample size, included BMA results present.  runtime_table data frame columns Method Runtime,       reporting computation time seconds method. total_runtime_seconds total computation time seconds across methods. ameras 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-26

title: ameras 0.2.0
text: Confidence intervals longer computed inside ameras(). arguments CI, params.profCI, maxit.profCI, tol.profCI deprecated removed version 1.0.0. Use new confint() method instead. See ?confint.amerasfit details. direct argument interface ameras() deprecated removed version 1.0.0. arguments Y, dosevars, doseRRmod, deg, M, X, offset, entry, exit, setnr deprecated. Please use new formula interface instead. See ?ameras details.

title: ameras 0.2.0
text: Implemented formula interface ameras(). dose variable specified using special dose() term, supports tidyselect syntax selecting dose columns allows specifying dose-response model effect modifiers directly formula. See ?ameras details examples. Added confint.amerasfit() computing confidence intervals separately model fitting. See ?confint.amerasfit details. Added function ecdfplot() exploratory visualization dose realizations model fitting.

title: ameras 0.2.0
text: Reduced memory usage large datasets. Removed use N x N matrix ERC Poisson family, improving memory computation speed. Removed internal duplication data RC ERC families. summary.amerasfit() now includes confidence interval columns computed via confint(). calling confint(), note printed directing user compute confidence intervals. Profile likelihood confidence interval bounds now include p-values summary table, making easier assess accuracy root-finding algorithm. Removed memoization profile likelihood function causing issues likely providing much benefit.

title: ameras 0.2.0
text: keep.data added ameras() (default TRUE). TRUE, data stored returned amerasfit object, required profile likelihood confidence interval computation via confint() without re-supplying data. Set FALSE reduce memory usage large datasets, case data must supplied confint() explicitly. See ?ameras ?confint.amerasfit details.
